source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 5.7 tree & 5.8 stat (pp. 80-82)

questions.q: What does the tree command do?
options: Expands the files and directories within a directory in a tree-like layout
Counts the inodes in a file system
questions.why: tree lists the contents of a directory as an indented tree of files and subdirectories.

questions.q: In tree, which options show file sizes human-readably, and print the protections (permissions)?
questions.why: -h prints sizes human-readably and -p prints each file's protections; -u shows the owner (UID), -g the group (GID), -a all files.

questions.q: Show /etc/yum.repos.d/ as a tree displaying protections, owner, group and human-readable sizes (combine -h -u -g -p).
questions.why: The short options can be grouped: -h sizes, -u owner, -g group, -p protections.

questions.q: What does the stat command display?
options: The status of a file or file system (size, inode, links, permissions, timestamps, ...)
The status of a systemd service
questions.why: stat reports detailed status for a file or file system, including its timestamps.

questions.q: Display the full status of the file /root/anaconda-ks.cfg.
questions.why: stat <file> prints its size, blocks, device, inode, links, permissions and the atime/mtime/ctime timestamps.

questions.q: In stat's output, what does ctime (Change) represent?
options: The last time the file's PROPERTIES changed (owner, group, permissions, size, links)
The last time the file's content was read
The time the file will next be backed up
questions.why: ctime is updated when metadata changes (owner/group/permissions/size/links); the creation time is the separate 'Birth' (btime) field, and content reads update atime.

questions.q: What is the block-size caveat between 'stat' and 'ls -ls'?
options: stat counts blocks as 512 bytes each, whereas ls -ls uses 1024-byte blocks
stat uses 1024 bytes; ls -ls uses 512 bytes
questions.why: The 'Blocks' figure from stat is in 512-byte units, while ls -ls reports blocks in 1024-byte units - so the numbers differ for the same file.
